primary_legislation.all: Electricity at Work Regulations 1989; Health and Safety at Work Act 1974

notes: Steps are identical to school MOD-005. Responsible person updated to Practice Manager. PAT is particularly important in GP practices given the volume of Class I medical and IT equipment in use.

logbooks.title: Monthly Electrical Visual Inspection
logbooks.frequency_detail: Once per calendar month. Informal visual check — not an engineering inspection.

steps.description: Walk the premises and visually inspect all accessible electrical installations and equipment: sockets, switches, fused spurs, distribution boards (external condition only — do not open), extension leads, and visible wiring.

Check for: damaged sockets or switches, overloaded sockets (multiple adapters or extension leads daisy-chained), visible damage to cables or plugs, signs of heat damage or burning, equipment placed near water sources.
Check all extension leads and multi-way adaptors: confirm they are fused, are not coiled (fire risk), and are not used as a permanent installation.
steps.photo_guidance: Photograph any overloaded socket, damaged cable, or daisy-chained extension leads.

steps.description: Any defect presenting an immediate danger must be isolated and taken out of use until repaired by a competent electrician.
Record: date, areas inspected, any defects found, action taken, inspector name.

logbooks.title: Annual Portable Appliance Testing (PAT)
logbooks.frequency_detail: Frequency is risk-based per IET Code of Practice. Annual is appropriate for most GP practice environments. Medical electrical equipment should follow manufacturer guidance and MHRA guidance — see GP-MOD-024 for medical devices.

steps.description: Arrange PAT with a competent person. Compile a list of all portable electrical appliances: computers, monitors, printers, kettles, fridges, hand-held equipment, extension leads, phone chargers, fan heaters.

Note: Class II medical electrical devices (double-insulated, used for patient contact) are covered in GP-MOD-024 and require additional checks per MHRA guidance. Include them in the PAT record but cross-reference.
Competent person carries out visual inspection and earth continuity/insulation resistance testing on Class I appliances per IET Code of Practice.
Each appliance that passes is labelled with the test date and next test date. Failed appliances are removed from service and not returned until repaired and re-tested.
Retain the PAT register. Record: date, tester name and competence, appliances tested, any failures, action taken.

logbooks.title: 5-Yearly Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR)
logbooks.frequency_detail: At minimum every 5 years (BS 7671 / IET Wiring Regulations recommendation for commercial premises). An EICR should also be obtained on change of occupancy.

steps.description: Arrange an EICR with a competent electrician (NICEIC, NAPIT, SELECT (Scotland), or NICEIC registered). The inspection covers the fixed wiring installation.

Electrician carries out the inspection and testing to BS 7671 18th edition (or current edition). The EICR report identifies: C1 (danger present — must remedy immediately), C2 (potentially dangerous — must remedy promptly), C3 (improvement recommended).
All C1 and C2 items must be rectified by a competent electrician before the premises can continue to be used (C1) or within a reasonable period (C2 — typically within 28 days for a GP surgery).

Retain the EICR and the completion certificate for any remedial works. Record: date, electrician name and scheme, outcome (satisfactory/unsatisfactory), C1/C2/C3 codes identified, remedial works carried out, next EICR due.
